message.content:
Adversarially review a generated HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (HLD) interview session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/e20d25e8-c990-40f3-9f7c-87018d8fc5bd_hld/_response.md  (+ maybe capacity.py)
Problem: Design Instagram. Emphasis was: feed fan-out (write vs read hybrid), blob storage + CDN for media, the celebrity fan-out problem (millions of followers), read-heavy ratio. Deep dives: feed generation at scale, hot-key celebrity, media upload pipeline + CDN, consistency of likes/counts.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (section / line) on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and FULLY EXPANDED (not stubs): §1 Requirements, §2 Clarifying Qs, §3 Scale & Capacity, §4 Entities, §5 API, §6 High-Level Design, §7 Data Model & Storage, §8 Deep Dives Bad→Good→Great, §9 Reliability/Failure/Cost, §10 Trade-off Ledger, §11 Interviewer Q&A. md length > 20,000 chars.
2. CAPACITY MATH (§3): are the numbers actually derived (visible derivation chains) and self-consistent (the arithmetic checks out)? Is there a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold? Any false precision or hand-waving? Re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present and confirm numbers match.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS: is the architecture sound for THIS problem and the emphasized focus? Are the deep dives actually the hardest problems (the binding bottleneck, hot-key/celebrity, the global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, idempotency on mutating endpoints)? Find any claim that is wrong or hand-wavy (e.g. "just add a cache" without the miss-QPS reasoning, oversell not actually prevented, consistency hand-waved).
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — HLD has many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block. FAIL any that contains a label/edge-text with breaking characters: parentheses ( ), square brackets used wrongly, a colon ':' inside an edge/label, a semicolon ';' inside a label, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, or curly braces in node/edge text. These break the renderer. Report the exact offending line per diagram. Also confirm diagrams are flowchart TD (not LR) for multi-path architecture.
5. API (§5): real REST endpoints with JSON examples, idempotency on mutating endpoints, security (caller identity from token)?
6. Data model (§7): stores chosen with the access-pattern justification, partition/shard keys, per-operation consistency?
7. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tool/XML tags, tables well-formed, the prescribed emoji-script structure present.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Mermaid breaking-char issues are BLOCKER (they break rendering). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

content.content:
1	# Design Instagram — High-Level Design
2	
3	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the feed fan-out, the celebrity hot-key, and the media pipeline — checking in with you as I go. I'll target an Amazon-style bar, so I'll keep cost and operations first-class and work backwards from what the user actually feels. Sound good?"
4	
5	The core tension of Instagram is this: it is one of the most **read-heavy** systems ever built — people scroll far more than they post — and most of every byte served is **media** (photos and short videos), not text. So the design lives or dies on two things: how cheaply and fast we can assemble a personalized **home feed**, and how cheaply we can store and serve **blobs** through a CDN. The villain of the whole story is the **celebrity** — one post that has to reach hundreds of millions of feeds.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)
10	
11	> 💬 "Let me frame the core tension first: this is a read-heavy, media-heavy social graph. The interesting problems aren't the upload itself — they're assembling a personalized feed at hundreds of thousands of reads a second, and serving petabytes of media without going bankrupt on bandwidth."
12	
13	### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")
14	
15	**1. Upload media (create a post).** A user uploads one or more photos/short videos with a caption.
16	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Someone picks a photo from their phone, adds a caption, taps share, and within a second or two it shows up on their profile and starts heading out to their followers."
17	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the file is large and goes to blob storage, but it also needs server-side transcoding into multiple sizes, and the moment it's saved we have to decide who gets it in their feed — that fan-out is the whole game.
18	
19	**2. Follow / unfollow users.** A user follows others to build their feed graph.
20	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You tap follow on someone and from then on their posts can appear in your home feed."
21	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the follow graph is enormously skewed — most people follow a few hundred accounts, but a few accounts are followed by hundreds of millions. That skew is what breaks naive designs.
22	
23	**3. Home feed (timeline of followees' posts).** A user opens the app and sees a reverse-chronological-ish, ranked feed of recent posts from people they follow.
24	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You open the app and instantly see the latest posts from everyone you follow, freshest and most relevant near the top."
25	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is the read hot path at ~350K requests/s peak. We can't query every followee's posts on every open at that scale, so we pre-compute — but pre-computing for a celebrity's 400M followers is a write bomb.
26	
27	**4. Like and comment on posts (with counts).** A user likes/comments; everyone sees the counts.
28	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You double-tap to like, the heart count ticks up, and others see roughly how many people liked it."
29	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** a viral post can take hundreds of thousands of likes per second — you cannot do `UPDATE posts SET likes = likes + 1` on a single row at that rate; that single row is a hot key.
30	
31	### Below the line (out of scope)
32	
33	- Stories / Reels ranking ML, Explore/Discovery feed, DMs, ads & monetization, live video, search, notifications fan-out (mention only), content moderation ML pipeline (we'll assume an async scanning hook), and the full ranking model (we'll treat ranking as a pluggable scorer).
34	
35	### Non-Functional
36	
37	**A. Feed read latency — p99 < 200 ms.** Home feed open must feel instant.
38	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When you open the app, 99 out of 100 times the feed is on screen in under a fifth of a second."
39	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the app feels laggy, engagement drops, and a slow feed is the single most-felt failure in the whole product.
40	
41	**B. Availability — feed/read path 99.99%; write/upload path 99.9%.** Reads must almost never fail.
42	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Four nines means the read path is down less than about 52 minutes a year total; three nines on uploads means under ~8.7 hours a year — uploads can retry, reads can't wait."
43	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if the feed errors out, hundreds of millions of sessions hit a blank screen — that's the headline incident.
44	
45	**C. Durability of media & posts — 11 nines (no lost photos).** A user's photos must never disappear.
46	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once you see 'posted,' that photo is effectively never lost — the chance of losing it is astronomically small."
47	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** losing user memories is unforgivable and the top trust-killer for a photo product.
48	
49	**D. Feed freshness — new posts visible within seconds to minutes.** Eventual consistency is fine for the feed.
50	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you post now, your followers see it within a few seconds normally, and within a minute or two even for a celebrity — it doesn't have to be instant for everyone."
51	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if we demanded instant consistency for every follower we'd be forced into synchronous fan-out and the write path would melt.
52	
53	**E. Cost efficiency — CDN egress is the budget.** The design must be operable at a sane $/month.
54	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Serving the images is the biggest bill by far — bigger than all the databases combined — so every design choice that reduces bytes-over-the-wire is worth real money."
55	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** at ~900 PB/month of egress, a careless rendition or cache strategy turns an $18M/mo bill into a $50M/mo bill.
56	
57	> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** ranking quality (the ML model) and abuse/moderation are real, hard, and I'm treating them as pluggable hooks here, not solving them — I'll call that out in the ledger.
58	
59	> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at the core, users upload media, follow people, read a home feed, and like/comment. The system is overwhelmingly read-heavy and media-heavy. My non-functionals are: feed reads under 200 ms at p99, four-nines on the read path, eleven-nines durability so we never lose a photo, feed freshness measured in seconds-to-minutes — eventual consistency is fine here — and cost, because CDN egress dwarfs everything else. I'm scoping out stories, DMs, ads, and the ranking model itself, treating ranking as a pluggable scorer."
60	
61	---
62	
63	## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions
64	
65	**Q1 — "What's the scale — how many daily active users, and what's the read-to-write ratio you're expecting?"**
66	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Because if it's a million users I build a monolith and go home; if it's hundreds of millions and 99% of traffic is reads, the entire design bends toward pre-computing feeds and caching media."
67	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If LARGE (100s of millions DAU) → fan-out + heavy caching + CDN, sharding everywhere. If SMALL → single Postgres, compute feed on read, skip the fan-out machinery entirely.
68	- **Assumption:** Instagram-scale — ~500M DAU, ~2B accounts, read:write around 300:1.
69	
70	**Q2 — "How fresh does the feed need to be? Is reverse-chronological enough, or do we need ML ranking?"**
71	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Freshness decides whether I can fan-out on write — pre-build feeds — or whether ranking forces me to assemble fresh at read time."
72	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If seconds-to-minutes freshness + simple ranking is OK → fan-out-on-write works. If we need real-time, per-request heavy ML ranking → I lean toward read-time assembly with a candidate-generation + ranking layer.
73	- **Assumption:** Freshness of seconds-to-minutes is fine; ranking is a re-ordering step on top of a pre-assembled candidate set.
74	
75	**Q3 — "How extreme is the follower skew — are there accounts with hundreds of millions of followers?"**
76	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "This is the make-or-break question. If everyone has a few hundred followers, naive fan-out works. If a few accounts have 400 million followers, fan-out-on-write for them is a write bomb and I need a hybrid."
77	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If skew is extreme (yes) → hybrid: fan-out-on-write for normal users, fan-out-on-read (pull) for celebrities. If skew is flat → pure fan-out-on-write.
78	- **Assumption:** Extreme skew — top accounts have hundreds of millions of followers. Hybrid is mandatory.
79	
80	**Q4 — "Photos only, or short videos too? And do we do server-side transcoding?"**
81	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Video changes storage and bandwidth by an order of magnitude and forces an async transcoding pipeline; it dominates the cost conversation."
82	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If video → async transcode pipeline + adaptive bitrate renditions + bigger CDN bill. If photo-only → simpler, just generate thumbnails.
83	- **Assumption:** Mostly photos (~80%) plus short videos (~20%); server-side transcoding into multiple renditions.
84	
85	**Q5 — "Do like/comment counts need to be exact, or is 'approximately 1.2M likes' acceptable?"**
86	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Exact counts on a viral post mean a single hot row taking 100K+ writes/s — impossible on one row. Approximate counts let me shard the counter."
87	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If approximate is OK → sharded/probabilistic counters, eventual. If exact required → much harder, materialized aggregation with reconciliation.
88	- **Assumption:** Approximate, eventually-consistent counts are fine for display; the user's own like state must be read-your-own-writes correct.
89	
90	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — Instagram-scale, extreme follower skew, video included, eventual feed and counts. Are you good with me proceeding on those, or do you want to pin any of them differently before I size it?"
91	
92	---
93	
94	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
95	
96	> First, glosses: **DAU** — daily active users, people who open the app on a given day. **QPS** — queries per second, how many requests hit the backend each second. **Fan-out** — copying one post into many followers' feeds. **Egress** — bytes leaving our network to users' devices (what the CDN bills us for). I computed these with a `capacity.py` I ran; below are the rounded results and the math that produced them.
97	
98	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
99	|---|---|---|---|---|
100	| Feed read QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 20 feed fetches/day = 10B reads/day; ÷ 86,400 s ≈ 116K/s avg; x3 peak ≈ 347K/s | ~350K/s | "about 350 thousand feed reads a second" | This is THE hot path — pre-compute + cache, don't query live |
101	| Post write QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 5% post x 1.2 posts = 30M/day; ÷ 86,400 ≈ 347/s avg; x3 ≈ 1K/s | ~1K/s | "about a thousand posts a second" | Writes are tiny — we can afford expensive work per write |
102	| Read:write ratio | 10B reads ÷ 30M writes | ~300:1 | "roughly 300 reads for every write" | Spend the budget on reads; do work on the write side to make reads cheap |
103	| Naive fan-out inserts (peak) | 30M posts/day x 200 avg followers = 6B inserts/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 69K/s avg; x3 ≈ 208K/s | ~210K/s | "about 200 thousand feed inserts a second" | Fan-out-on-write is feasible for normal users |
104	| Celebrity single-post fan-out | one post x 400M followers | ~400M inserts | "one celeb post is 400 million feed writes" | Forces the hybrid — NEVER fan-out a celebrity on write |
105	| Like QPS (peak) | 500M DAU x 10 likes/day = 5B/day; ÷86,400 ≈ 58K/s; x3 ≈ 174K/s | ~175K/s | "about 175 thousand likes a second" | Counters must be sharded, never a single hot row |
106	| Media ingested/year | 30M posts/day x ~4.2 MB blended x 365, x1.4 durability | ~64 PB/yr | "tens of petabytes of new media a year" | Object store (S3-class) + lifecycle tiering, not a DB |
107	| CDN egress/month | 10B reads x 10 posts/page x 0.3 MB display = 30 PB/day; x30 ≈ 900 PB/mo | ~900 PB/mo | "roughly 900 petabytes out the door a month" | The dominant cost line — ~$18M/mo; optimize bytes-on-wire |
108	| Feed-store miss QPS (peak) | 347K/s peak x (1 − 0.95 hit) ≈ 17K/s residual to the store | ~17K/s | "about 17 thousand reads a second hit the store" | A handful of cache shards absorb the read tide |
109	
110	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **celebrity single-post fan-out of ~400M writes**. Its flip threshold: if no account exceeded roughly **the point where fan-out latency blows past our freshness SLO** — practically, more than a few hundred thousand to a million followers — we'd push it as a write. Above that, it must be a **pull (read-time merge)**. That single fact is why the architecture is a **hybrid**, not pure push.
111	
112	> Storage of post *metadata* is a non-issue — about 30 TB/year with replication, a rounding error next to media. The real money is **CDN egress (~$18M/mo)** and **media at rest (~64 PB/yr)**.
113	
114	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is the read tide — about 350 thousand feed reads a second at peak against only about a thousand writes, so call it 300 reads to every write. That tells me to spend everything making reads cheap and do the expensive work on the write side. The second number that drives the whole design is the celebrity: one post from a 400-million-follower account is 400 million feed writes — so I will never fan that out on write. That's why I'm going hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrities."
115	
116	---
117	
118	## 4. Core Entities
119	
120	> This is my first-draft list; I'll add full fields in the data model.
121	
122	**User** — owns identity, profile, and follower/following counts.
123	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of it like a person's library card — it identifies them and tracks what they're subscribed to."
124	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you store follower counts for someone with 400M followers?" → "Not a live `COUNT(*)` — a denormalized, eventually-consistent counter updated asynchronously."
125	
126	**Follow (edge)** — owns the directed graph edge `follower → followee`.
127	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a one-way subscription: I subscribe to your channel; you don't automatically get mine."
128	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "How do you find all followers of a celebrity fast?" → "Indexed by followee; but we deliberately don't iterate them on every celeb post — that's the pull path."
129	
130	**Post** — owns caption, author, timestamp, and references to media blobs (not the bytes).
131	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a catalog card pointing at the actual photo in the warehouse — the card is tiny, the warehouse is the blob store."
132	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Where do the photo bytes live?" → "In object storage; the Post row only holds blob keys and rendition URLs, never the bytes."
133	
134	**Media (blob + renditions)** — owns the actual file and its transcoded sizes (thumbnail, feed, full).
135	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "The warehouse shelf holding the original photo plus several pre-cut sizes ready to ship."
136	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Why multiple renditions?" → "So the feed serves a 0.3 MB thumbnail, not a 5 MB original — that's most of the CDN savings."
137	
138	**FeedEntry (timeline cache)** — owns a single user's pre-computed list of `(post_id, score, ts)`.
139	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Like a personalized mailbox we keep pre-stuffed so opening the app is just reading your mailbox, not searching the whole post office."
140	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Is this the source of truth?" → "No — it's a derived cache; we can always rebuild it from Posts + Follows."
141	
142	**Interaction (Like / Comment)** — owns who liked/commented on what, plus the aggregate counters.
143	- 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "A guestbook entry plus a running tally on the cover."
144	- ⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "Exact or approximate counts?" → "Sharded counters, approximate for display; the user's own like state is exact via a per-user set."
145	
146	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are User, the Follow edge, Post, Media, the FeedEntry timeline cache, and Interactions for likes and comments. The one I'd flag as the most important and most misunderstood is FeedEntry — it's not a source of truth, it's a derived, pre-computed mailbox we can always rebuild. I'll fill in the full field-level schema in the data-model section."
147	
148	---
149	
150	## 5. API / Interface
151	
152	> Default REST over HTTPS. The caller is always identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token in the request header that proves who you are — the server trusts the token, never a user id in the body)*. We never trust client-supplied ids, timestamps, or counts.
153	
154	**5.1 Upload a post** — `POST /v1/posts`
155	- *Why POST:* it creates new server state (a new post).
156	- Key request fields: `mediaUploadIds: string[]` — references to already-uploaded blobs (see 5.2); `caption: string`; `idempotencyKey: string` — dedups retries so a flaky network can't create two posts. The client does **not** send `authorId` (taken from the JWT) or `createdAt` (server clock).
157	```
158	// Request
159	POST /v1/posts
160	Headers: Authorization: Bearer <jwt>, Idempotency-Key: 9f2c-uuid
161	{ "mediaUploadIds": ["upl_a1", "upl_a2"], "caption": "sunset 🌅" }
162	
163	// Response 202
164	{ "postId": "post_7Yk", "status": "PROCESSING", "createdAt": "2026-06-21T18:04:00Z" }
165	```
166	- **Key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted / PROCESSING**, not 200 — transcoding and fan-out are async. `authorId` and `status` are server-set; the client can't forge them.
167	
168	**5.2 Get an upload URL** — `POST /v1/media:initUpload`
169	- *Why POST:* it allocates a server resource (a signed upload slot).
170	- Returns a **pre-signed URL** *(a temporary, permissioned link that lets the phone upload bytes straight to object storage, bypassing our servers)*.
171	```
172	// Request
173	POST /v1/media:initUpload
174	{ "contentType": "image/jpeg", "byteSize": 4200000 }
175	
176	// Response 200
177	{ "uploadId": "upl_a1", "uploadUrl": "https://blob.cdn/...sig...", "expiresIn": 600 }
178	```
179	- **Key design decision:** the bytes never traverse our app servers — the phone PUTs directly to the blob store. This is the single biggest scalability win on the write path.
180	
181	**5.3 Follow a user** — `PUT /v1/users/{targetId}/follow`
182	- *Why PUT:* idempotent — following twice is the same as following once.
183	```
184	// Request:  PUT /v1/users/u_celeb42/follow
185	// Response 200
186	{ "following": true, "since": "2026-06-21T18:04:10Z" }
187	```
188	- **Key design decision:** idempotent by nature, so no idempotency key needed; `followerId` comes from the JWT.
189	
190	**5.4 Get home feed** — `GET /v1/feed?cursor=<opaque>&limit=20`
191	- *Why GET:* read-only.
192	- Key params: `cursor` — opaque pagination token (encodes position + freshness watermark) so infinite scroll is stable even as new posts arrive; `limit`.
193	```
194	// Request:  GET /v1/feed?cursor=eyJ0cyI6MTcuLi59&limit=20
195	// Response 200
196	{ "items": [
197	    { "postId": "post_7Yk", "authorId": "u_99", "caption": "sunset 🌅",
198	      "media": [{ "url": "https://cdn/feed/post_7Yk_a1.jpg", "w": 1080 }],
199	      "likeCount": 1243, "youLiked": false, "createdAt": "2026-06-21T18:04:00Z" }
200	  ],
201	  "nextCursor": "eyJ0cyI6MTcuLi4xfQ" }
202	```
203	- **Key design decision:** the server merges the pre-computed push feed with freshly-pulled celebrity posts here; `youLiked` is resolved per-caller so the feed is read-your-own-writes correct for your own like state.
204	
205	**5.5 Like a post** — `PUT /v1/posts/{postId}/like`
206	- *Why PUT:* idempotent — liking twice still means "liked once."
207	```
208	// Request:  PUT /v1/posts/post_7Yk/like
209	// Response 200
210	{ "liked": true, "likeCount": 1244 }
211	```
212	- **Key design decision:** idempotent on `(userId, postId)`; the returned `likeCount` is approximate (sharded counter), but `liked` is exact for the caller.
213	
214	> 💬 "Notice the client never sends authorId, price-equivalents, or counts — those all come from the server. The phone uploads bytes straight to blob storage via a pre-signed URL, and post creation returns 202 because the real work is async."
215	
216	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The API is plain REST. To post, the phone first asks for a pre-signed upload URL and PUTs the bytes straight to object storage — they never hit our app servers, which is the big write-path win. Then it calls POST /posts with just the upload ids and a caption; we take the author from the JWT, return 202 PROCESSING, and do transcoding and fan-out asynchronously. Follow and like are idempotent PUTs. Reading the feed is a GET with an opaque cursor, and we resolve your own like-state per-request so you always see your own actions reflected."

220	## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)
221	
222	> Color language used in every diagram: blue = client apps, green = our services, amber = queues/streams, grey = datastores (cylinders), purple = external (CDN/object store).
223	
224	### 6.1 "Upload media (create a post)"
225	
226	The interesting part isn't storing a file — it's getting big bytes off the app servers and kicking off async transcode + fan-out.
227	
228	**Architecture decisions**
229	
230	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
231	|---|---|---|---|---|
232	| Object store (S3-class) | A bucket that stores arbitrarily large files cheaply and durably | 11-nines durability + pre-signed direct upload keeps petabytes off our DB and our app servers | Not Postgres BLOBs (a DB is the wrong place for 64 PB; bloats backups); not NFS (no global durability) | Eventual visibility of new keys; no transactions across objects |
233	| Upload service | A thin service that mints pre-signed URLs and records post metadata | Lets the phone upload directly, so our servers only handle tiny metadata | Not proxying bytes through app (would need huge bandwidth + memory) | Must validate the object exists/scan it after the fact |
234	| Post metadata DB | A sharded relational store for post rows | Need indexed queries by author and time, modest size (~30 TB/yr) | Not a pure KV (we query by author + time ranges) | Manual sharding by author_id |
235	| Media-processing queue | A durable log of "new media uploaded" events | Decouples slow transcoding from the fast user response | Not synchronous transcode (would block the 202 for seconds) | Feed/profile shows a placeholder until transcode finishes |
236	| Transcode workers | A pool that makes thumbnail/feed/full renditions | One 5 MB original becomes a 0.3 MB feed image — most CDN savings | Not on-the-fly resize at serve time (recompute every view = waste) | Storage of several renditions per post |
237	
238	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
239	> **Object store (S3-class)** — a service that stores files (blobs) by key with extreme durability and cheap-per-GB pricing. Here it holds every photo/video and all renditions, so the DB never touches bytes.
240	> **Pre-signed URL** — a temporary signed link that grants the holder permission to upload one object directly. Here it lets phones bypass our servers entirely on upload.
241	> **Queue (durable log)** — a buffer that reliably holds events until a worker consumes them. Here it holds "media uploaded" events so transcoding can happen out of band without making the user wait.
242	> **Transcoding** — converting a media file into other sizes/formats. Here it produces the small feed rendition that saves most of the bandwidth bill.
243	
244	**Diagram — Upload / create-post flow**
245	
246	```mermaid
247	flowchart TD
248	    Client[Client app]
249	    Client -->|"1 initUpload"| Upload[Upload service]
250	    Upload -->|"2 presigned url"| Client
251	    Client -->|"3 PUT bytes"| Blob[(Object store)]
252	    Client -->|"4 POST post meta"| Upload
253	    Upload -->|"5 write row"| PostDB[(Post metadata DB)]
254	    Upload -.->|"6 emit media event"| MQ[[Media queue]]
255	    MQ -.->|"7 consume"| Worker[Transcode workers]
256	    Worker -->|"8 write renditions"| Blob
257	    Worker -.->|"9 emit post-ready"| FanQ[[Fan-out queue]]
258	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
259	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
260	    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3

270	**Numbered step narration**
271	1. **Client calls initUpload.** It goes to the Upload service because that service owns minting signed slots. State: an `uploadId` is reserved. If it fails, the client simply retries — nothing durable yet.
272	2. **Service returns a pre-signed URL.** Why here: only the server holds the signing key. State: none yet. Risk: an expired URL → client re-inits.
273	3. **Client PUTs bytes straight to the object store.** Why direct: keeps 64 PB/yr of bytes off our app servers. State: the blob now exists. Risk: partial upload → the object is incomplete; we only reference it after step 4 validates size.
274	4. **Client POSTs post metadata** (upload ids + caption). Why Upload service: it owns post rows. State: a Post row in `PROCESSING`. Risk: duplicate POST → the idempotency key dedups.
275	5. **Service writes the Post row** to the sharded metadata DB, sharded by author. State: durable post record. Risk: shard down → retry with backoff; client already has its 202.
276	6. **Service emits a "media uploaded" event** to the media queue (fire-and-forget). State: event durably enqueued. Risk: queue down → upload service buffers/retries; user still sees PROCESSING.
277	7–8. **Transcode workers consume and write renditions** (thumbnail/feed/full) back to the object store. State: renditions exist; post can be marked READY. Risk: a bad file → mark FAILED, notify user to retry.
278	9. **Worker emits "post-ready"** to the fan-out queue — this hands off to §6.3.
279	
280	> 💬 "The trick here is the bytes never touch my servers — the phone uploads straight to the bucket, and everything slow happens after a fast 202."
281	
282	> 🎙️ **Script:** "On upload, the phone first grabs a pre-signed URL and uploads the bytes directly to object storage — that keeps tens of petabytes a year completely off my app servers. Then it posts just the metadata, I write a tiny post row, return 202 immediately, and drop an event on a queue. Transcode workers pick that up, make the small renditions that save most of my bandwidth bill, and then signal that the post is ready to fan out. The trade-off is the post is briefly in a PROCESSING state, which is invisible to the user in practice."
283	
284	### 6.2 "Follow / unfollow"
285	
286	A tiny write, but it's the input to feed assembly and it tells us whether the target is a celebrity.
287	
288	**Architecture decisions**
289	
290	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
291	|---|---|---|---|---|
292	| Graph/edge store | A store of directed follow edges, indexed both ways | Need "who do I follow" (feed build) and "who follows X" (fan-out) | Not a graph DB (overkill — we only need 1-hop edges); not a single index (need both directions) | Two indexes to keep in sync |
293	| Follower-count counter | A denormalized, async-updated tally per user | Live COUNT over 400M edges is impossible per request | Not on-read COUNT(*) (scans millions of rows) | Counts are eventually consistent |
294	| Celebrity flag | A boolean/threshold marking high-follower accounts | Lets the feed pick push vs pull per author | Not recomputed per post (cache the flag) | Flag lags reality by minutes near the threshold |
295	
296	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
297	> **Edge store** — a store of relationship rows (follower → followee) indexed in both directions. Here it answers both "whose posts go in my feed" and "who should receive a normal user's post."
298	> **Denormalized counter** — a stored running total updated asynchronously instead of computed on demand. Here it serves the follower count without scanning the edge table.
299	
300	**Diagram — Follow flow**
301	
302	```mermaid
303	flowchart TD
304	    Client[Client app]
305	    Client -->|"1 PUT follow"| FollowSvc[Follow service]
306	    FollowSvc -->|"2 upsert edge"| Edge[(Edge store)]
307	    FollowSvc -.->|"3 incr counters"| Counter[(Counter store)]
308	    FollowSvc -->|"4 check threshold"| CelebFlag[(Celebrity flag)]
309	    FollowSvc -.->|"5 backfill recent posts"| FeedCache[(Feed cache)]
310	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
311	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
312	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
313	    class Client client

317	↳ reuses existing: Feed cache (introduced in §6.3).
318	
319	**Numbered step narration**
320	1. **Client PUTs follow.** Goes to the Follow service which owns edges. State: a follow request. Risk: double-tap → idempotent upsert.
321	2. **Upsert the edge** (both directions indexed). State: graph edge exists. Risk: partial write → retry; edge store is the source of truth.
322	3. **Increment follower/following counters** async. State: counts trend correct. Risk: lost increment → periodic reconciliation job.
323	4. **Check the celebrity threshold** for the followee. State: maybe flip the celeb flag. Risk: thundering follows near threshold → debounce the flag flip.
324	5. **Optionally backfill** the follower's feed cache with the followee's recent posts so the feed isn't empty right after following. State: feed cache warmed. Risk: skip for celebrities (we pull them at read time anyway).
325	
326	> 💬 "Following is trivial mechanically, but it's where I decide push-vs-pull — by checking if the target is a celebrity."
327	
328	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Follow is a small idempotent write into an edge store I index in both directions — one direction builds your feed, the other tells me who should receive a normal user's posts. I bump denormalized counters asynchronously instead of counting rows live, and I check a cached celebrity flag, because that flag is what later decides whether a post gets pushed to followers or pulled at read time."

330	### 6.3 "Home feed (timeline of followees' posts)"
331	
332	This is the read hot path at ~350K/s and the heart of the design: a **hybrid** of push (pre-computed) and pull (live-merged) feeds.
333	
334	**Architecture decisions**
335	
336	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
337	|---|---|---|---|---|
338	| Feed cache (Redis) | An in-memory store holding each user's pre-built timeline list | Reading your mailbox is O(1) and sub-ms; absorbs the 350K/s tide | Not query-on-read for everyone (would hammer the DB at 350K/s) | Memory cost; it's a derived cache, must be rebuildable |
339	| Fan-out service + queue | Workers that copy a new post into followers' feed caches | Pre-computing on the cheap write side keeps reads trivial | Not synchronous fan-out (a celeb post would block) | Eventual feed freshness (seconds) |
340	| Feed assembly service | Merges the push list with freshly-pulled celebrity posts, then ranks | Hybrid avoids the celeb write bomb while keeping normal feeds pre-built | Not pure pull (would be too slow for everyone) | Read-time merge cost for celeb posts only |
341	| Ranking scorer (pluggable) | Re-orders candidate posts by a score | Lets us swap chrono → ML without touching the pipeline | Not hard-coded chrono (product wants ranking) | Treated as a black box here |
342	
343	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
344	> **Fan-out-on-write (push)** — when you post, we immediately copy a reference into each follower's pre-built feed. Here it's used for normal users so reads are just a cache fetch.
345	> **Fan-out-on-read (pull)** — at feed-read time we fetch a few celebrities' recent posts live and merge them in. Here it avoids writing one celeb post to 400M feeds.
346	> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value/data-structure store, microsecond-fast. Here it holds each user's timeline as a sorted list keyed by user id.
347	
348	**Diagram — Hybrid feed (read + fan-out paths)**

377	
378	**Numbered step narration**
379	1. **Client GETs feed.** Hits the Feed assembly service, which owns merge+rank. State: none. Risk: cold start → fall through to a rebuild from edges.
380	2. **Read the pre-pushed list** from the feed cache (the user's mailbox). State: candidate set of normal-follow posts. Risk: cache miss → rebuild from edge store + recent posts.
381	3. **Pull recent posts of the user's followed celebrities** live from the post DB (a handful of authors). State: celeb candidates added. Risk: celeb DB hot → these are themselves cached per-celebrity (§8).
382	4. **Rank/merge** the combined candidates. State: ordered feed page. Risk: ranker slow/down → fall back to chronological.
383	5. **Hydrate media URLs** (rendition URLs, not bytes) and return. State: response sent. Risk: missing rendition → serve placeholder.
384	6–7. **Asynchronously**, when a normal user posts, the fan-out service consumes "post-ready" and **pushes the post id into each follower's feed cache list**. State: followers' mailboxes updated. Risk: a borderline-celebrity → skip push, rely on pull.
385	
386	> 💬 "The whole feed is a hybrid: I pre-stuff normal users' mailboxes on write, but I pull celebrity posts live at read time and merge — that one decision dodges the 400-million-write bomb."
387	
388	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The feed is a hybrid. For normal users I fan out on write — when you post, I copy a tiny reference into each follower's pre-built list in Redis, so reading the feed is just fetching your mailbox, sub-millisecond, which is how I survive 350 thousand reads a second. But for celebrities I do the opposite: I never push their posts; instead, when you open your feed I pull the handful of celebrities you follow live and merge them in. Then a pluggable ranker orders the page. The trade-off is the feed is eventually consistent — fresh within seconds — which the product is fine with."

435	## 7. Data Model & Storage
436	
437	### PART A — Entity table
438	
439	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Partition key | Consistency |
440	|---|---|---|---|---|
441	| User | **user_id (PK)**, handle, profile, follower_count, celeb_flag | Sharded relational | user_id | Strong for profile; eventual for counts |
442	| Follow edge | **(follower_id, followee_id) (PK)**, created_at; indexed both ways | Wide-column (Cassandra-class) | follower_id (and a mirror by followee_id) | Eventual |
443	| Post | **post_id (PK)**, **author_id (shard key)**, caption, media_keys, created_at, status | Sharded relational | author_id | Strong within author shard |
444	| Media/renditions | **media_key (PK)**, post_id, renditions[], bytes | Object store + metadata in post DB | media_key (hashed) | Eventual key visibility |
445	| FeedEntry | **user_id (PK)** → sorted list of (post_id, score, ts) | Redis (sorted set), rebuildable | user_id | Eventual (derived cache) |
446	| Like | **(user_id, post_id) (PK)**; aggregate like_count per post | Wide-column + sharded counter | user_id (set), post_id (counter) | Eventual count; exact per-user state |
447	| Comment | **comment_id (PK)**, post_id (shard), author_id, body, ts | Sharded relational/wide-column | post_id | Eventual |
448	
449	### PART B — Storage decision cards
450	
451	**🗄️ Object store (S3-class) — used for: Media bytes + all renditions**
452	1. **What it is:** a service that stores files (blobs) by key with 11-nines durability and very cheap per-GB pricing, with lifecycle tiering (hot → cold).
453	2. **Why we picked it — the forcing access pattern:** we ingest ~64 PB/year and must never lose a photo; we need cheap-per-GB durable bytes that the CDN can pull from as origin. No database can hold petabytes of blobs sanely.
454	3. **Considered & rejected:** **Postgres BLOBs** — would explode backup/replication and is the wrong tool for petabytes; **self-hosted distributed FS** — operationally heavy, no native CDN integration; **storing in the DB row** — bloats every read.
455	4. **Trade-off:** eventual key visibility and no cross-object transactions — fine, because media is immutable once written.
456	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Photos and videos go in S3-class object storage — eleven nines of durability, dirt cheap per gigabyte, and the CDN pulls from it as origin. A database is the wrong place for sixty-odd petabytes a year."
457	
458	**🗄️ Sharded relational (Post / User / Comment) — used for: metadata**
459	1. **What it is:** a relational database (Postgres-class) split across shards by a key, storing structured rows with indexes and transactions.
460	2. **Why we picked it — the forcing access pattern:** posts are queried by author and time range (profile pages, celebrity pull), and metadata is small (~30 TB/yr) — relational indexing fits perfectly.
461	3. **Considered & rejected:** **pure KV** — can't do author+time range scans efficiently; **single unsharded Postgres** — won't hold the write/index load at billions of rows; **graph DB** — overkill for 1-hop reads.
462	4. **Trade-off:** manual sharding by author_id; cross-shard queries need scatter-gather (rare here).
463	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Post and user metadata goes in a sharded relational store keyed by author — it's small, and I query it by author and time for profiles and celebrity pulls, which relational indexes nail."
464	
465	**🗄️ Redis (Feed cache) — used for: pre-computed timelines**
466	1. **What it is:** an in-memory data-structure store; here we use a sorted set per user keyed by user_id holding (post_id, score).
467	2. **Why we picked it — the forcing access pattern:** 350K feed reads/s need sub-ms mailbox reads; only in-memory can do that, and a sorted set gives ranked pagination for free.
468	3. **Considered & rejected:** **read-from-DB on every feed open** — would melt the post DB at 350K/s; **Memcached** — no native sorted structures for ranked pagination.
469	4. **Trade-off:** memory cost and it's volatile — but it's a derived cache we can always rebuild from edges + posts.
470	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Feeds live in Redis as a sorted set per user — it's the mailbox I pre-stuff, sub-millisecond to read, and if a node dies I just rebuild it from the follow graph and recent posts."
471	
472	**🗄️ Wide-column (Cassandra-class) — used for: follow edges, likes, comments**
473	1. **What it is:** a horizontally-scalable store optimized for huge write volume and simple key-based reads, with tunable consistency.
474	2. **Why we picked it — the forcing access pattern:** billions of edges/likes with high write throughput and partition-key reads ("who follows X", "did user like post"); writes massively outnumber complex queries.
475	3. **Considered & rejected:** **relational** — sharding billions of edges + counters is painful; **Redis only** — not durable enough as the source of truth for the graph.
476	4. **Trade-off:** eventual consistency and no joins — fine for these append-heavy, key-addressed entities.
477	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Follow edges, likes, and comments go in a wide-column store like Cassandra — it eats writes, scales horizontally, and I only ever read these by a partition key, so I don't need joins."
478	
479	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
480	
481	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why that level |
482	|---|---|---|---|
483	| Create post (metadata) | Sharded relational | Strong (within author shard) | The author must see their own post immediately |
484	| Media bytes written | Object store | Eventual key visibility | Media is immutable; brief delay is invisible |
485	| Fan-out into feeds | Redis | Eventual (seconds) | Feed freshness SLO is seconds-to-minutes |
486	| Read home feed | Redis + live pull | Eventual | Stale-by-seconds is acceptable; reads can't block |
487	| Like a post (your state) | Wide-column set | Read-your-own-writes | You must see your own heart fill instantly |
488	| Like count (display) | Sharded counter | Eventual / approximate | "1.2M likes" need not be exact |
489	| Follower count | Counter | Eventual | Display-only; reconciled periodically |
490	
491	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Storage splits four ways. The dominant store is S3-class object storage for the petabytes of media — durable and cheap, with the CDN in front. Post and user metadata is a sharded relational DB, because it's small and I query it by author and time. Feeds live in Redis as pre-built mailboxes for sub-millisecond reads. And the high-write, key-addressed stuff — follow edges, likes, comments — goes in a wide-column store like Cassandra. I considered just reading feeds from the post DB on every open, but that would melt it at 350 thousand reads a second, so the pre-computed Redis mailbox earns its place."

495	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
496	
497	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two anchors — "reads are 300x writes, so push work onto the write side" and "the celebrity is the villain." Every deep dive reduces to one of those.
498	
499	### How do we generate the home feed at scale?
500	
501	#### Bad: Query followees on every read
502	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Every time you open the app, go ask the database for the latest posts from everyone you follow and sort them."
503	**Approach:** on `GET /feed`, look up all followees from the edge store, query the post DB for their recent posts, merge-sort by time, return.
504	**Why people try this:** it's the obvious, correct, zero-precompute design — and it's what you'd build for a small app.
505	**⚠️ What breaks:** at 350K feed reads/s, each read fans into a scatter query across hundreds of followees' shards — that's tens of millions of post-DB queries per second. The post DB caps out; p99 blows past seconds. ⚠️ **Trap:** this is the classic "compute-on-read" mistake that sinks news-feed answers.
506	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the read:write ratio (300:1) — we're paying the expensive assembly cost on the frequent side. Move it to the rare side (writes).
507	
508	#### Good: Fan-out-on-write (push) into Redis mailboxes
509	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad recomputed the feed on every read; this pre-computes it once on write so reads are a single cache fetch.
510	**🗣️ Plain words:** "When you post, we immediately drop a copy into each follower's pre-built mailbox, so opening the app is just reading your mailbox."
511	**Approach:** on post-ready, a fan-out worker reads the author's followers and `LPUSH`/`ZADD`s the post id into each follower's Redis sorted set. Reads become one `ZREVRANGE` — sub-millisecond.
512	**Why people try this:** it perfectly exploits the 300:1 ratio — do work where it's rare.
513	**⚠️ What breaks:** the celebrity. One post by a 400M-follower account = **400M Redis writes** in a single fan-out job. At the math below, that job can't finish inside the freshness SLO and it floods the fan-out tier, delaying everyone else's posts too.
514	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any account above ~a few hundred K followers makes the push job too big — we need to NOT push those.
515	
516	```mermaid
517	flowchart TD
518	    PostReady[Post ready event]
519	    PostReady -->|"1 read followers"| Edge[(Edge store)]
520	    PostReady -->|"2 for each follower"| FanSvc[Fan-out workers]
521	    FanSvc -->|"3 ZADD post into mailbox"| FeedCache[(Feed cache Redis)]
522	    Reader[Feed read] -->|"4 ZREVRANGE mailbox"| FeedCache
523	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
524	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
525	    class FanSvc svc
526	    class PostReady,Reader svc
527	    class Edge,FeedCache db
528	```
529	
530	#### Great: Hybrid push/pull with a celebrity cut-off
531	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good pushed every author including celebrities, creating a 400M-write bomb; Great refuses to push high-fan-out authors and pulls them at read time instead.
532	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Push normal people's posts into mailboxes like before, but for the handful of celebrities you follow, fetch their latest posts live when you open the app and blend them in."
533	**Approach:** at post time, check the author's celeb_flag. **If normal** → fan-out-on-write as in Good. **If celebrity** → do nothing on write. At read time, the feed service reads the pushed mailbox AND issues a small live query for the user's followed celebrities' recent posts (each celebrity's recent posts are themselves cached once and shared by all readers — see hot-key dive), then ranks and merges. This caps both sides: writes never exceed the threshold, reads pull only a handful of celebrities.
534	**Why this is the answer:** it puts each author on the cheaper side of the 300:1 ratio for *their* fan-out shape.
535	
536	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** one celeb post = **400M** feed inserts. To deliver in 60s that's **~6.7M inserts/s from a single post** — no fan-out tier sustains that. Pull instead: a reader follows maybe ~50 celebrities; merging 50 cached lists at read time is trivial. Flip threshold: push while `followers x post_rate` keeps the fan-out under the freshness SLO — practically **~hundreds of thousands of followers**; above that, pull.
537	
538	**✅ Failure matrix**
539	| Scenario | What happens |
540	|---|---|
541	| Author normal, post created | Fan-out pushes to follower mailboxes within seconds |
542	| Author celebrity, post created | No push; appears via read-time pull, served from celeb cache |
543	| Reader's mailbox cache evicted | Rebuild from edge store + recent posts (cold path) |
544	| Borderline account crosses threshold | Stop pushing new posts; existing pushed posts stay; pull picks up the rest |
545	| Fan-out queue backed up | Posts land later but never lost (durable queue); reads degrade to more pull |
546	
547	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The feed is a hybrid. Normal users get fan-out-on-write — I copy their post into each follower's Redis mailbox, so reads are a single sub-millisecond fetch, which is how I take 350 thousand reads a second. But a celebrity post would be 400 million writes — about 6.7 million a second to hit a one-minute SLO — so I never push those. Instead, at read time I pull the handful of celebrities you follow from a shared per-celebrity cache and merge them in. Each author sits on whichever side of the read-write trade-off is cheaper for their fan-out shape."
548	> 🧠 **If they ask "where's the cutoff?":** "I push while followers times post-rate keeps the fan-out under my freshness SLO — practically a few hundred thousand followers. It's a tunable threshold with hysteresis so accounts near the line don't flap."
549	
550	### How do we handle the celebrity / hot-key problem on reads?
551	
552	#### Bad: Each reader pulls the celebrity's posts independently
553	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Everyone who follows the celebrity hits the post DB for their latest posts separately."
554	**Approach:** read-time pull queries the post DB directly per reader.
555	**⚠️ What breaks:** a celebrity followed by 400M people, all opening the app around a post → millions of identical queries hit ONE author shard. That single shard is a hot key; it tips over.
556	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the same query repeated millions of times should be answered once.
557	
558	#### Good: Per-celebrity read-through cache
559	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad let identical reads stampede one shard; Good answers them once and caches the result.
560	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Cache the celebrity's latest posts once; everyone reads the cached copy."
561	**Approach:** cache `celebrity_id → recent posts` in Redis with a short TTL. All readers hit the cache; the post DB sees ~1 query per TTL window per celebrity.
562	**⚠️ What breaks:** on a cache miss right after a post, **millions of readers miss simultaneously** and all fall through to the DB at once — a **cache stampede** (thundering herd).
563	
564	```mermaid
565	flowchart TD
566	    Readers[Many feed reads]
567	    Readers -->|"1 read celeb cache"| CelebCache[(Celeb post cache)]
568	    CelebCache -.->|"2 miss"| PostDB[(Post metadata DB)]
569	    PostDB -->|"3 fill cache"| CelebCache
570	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
571	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
572	    class Readers svc
573	    class CelebCache,PostDB db
574	```
575	
576	#### Great: Replicated cache + single-flight + active push to edge cache
577	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good still let a miss stampede the DB; Great collapses concurrent misses and replicates the hot key.
578	**🗣️ Plain words:** "When the cached copy expires, let exactly one request rebuild it while everyone else waits a beat for the fresh copy; and spread the hot celebrity across many cache replicas so no single node melts."
579	**Approach:** (1) **single-flight** *(only one request recomputes a missing key; concurrent callers wait for that one result)* on the celeb cache, so a miss triggers exactly one DB read. (2) **Replicate the hot key** across N cache replicas (or local in-process caches on each feed server) so the per-node QPS for one celebrity is divided by N. (3) On a celebrity post, **actively push** the new post into these caches so there's rarely a cold miss at all.
580	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** without caching, ~400M followers reading a celeb's posts ≈ a huge fraction of the 350K/s on one shard — instant death. With a shared cache + single-flight, the post DB sees **~1 read per celebrity per TTL window** (single digits/s total). With local caches on, say, 200 feed servers, even cache-tier QPS per node drops ~200x.
581	
582	**✅ Failure matrix**
583	| Scenario | What happens |
584	|---|---|
585	| Celeb cache hit | Served from memory, DB untouched |
586	| Cache expiry, many readers | Single-flight: one rebuilds, others wait ~ms for fresh value |
587	| Cache node dies | Replica/local cache serves; one node's loss isn't the whole hot key |
588	| New celeb post | Active push refreshes caches; no cold-miss stampede |
589	| Brand-new viral account | celeb_flag flips; subsequent reads pull-cached instead of pushed |
590	
591	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The celebrity is a hot key on reads too. I cache each celebrity's latest posts once and everyone reads that copy, so the post DB sees roughly one query per celebrity per cache window instead of millions. To stop a stampede when that cache expires, I use single-flight — one request rebuilds it while the rest wait a few milliseconds — and I replicate the hot key across many cache nodes, even local in-process caches on the feed servers, so no single node takes 400 million followers' worth of reads. And I actively push a new celeb post into those caches so there's rarely a cold miss at all."
592	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just push celebs too, with more workers?":** "Throwing workers at 400 million writes per post still bursts the fan-out tier and the freshness SLO; pull plus a shared cache answers the same demand with a few queries instead of hundreds of millions of writes."
593	
594	### How do we count likes on a viral post without a hot row?
595	
596	#### Bad: `UPDATE posts SET likes = likes + 1`
597	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Add one to a single counter column on the post each time."
598	**Approach:** a single row holds the like count; every like is an UPDATE.
599	**⚠️ What breaks:** a viral post can take ~175K likes/s system-wide and tens of thousands on one post — all hitting ONE row. Row-level lock contention serializes them; the shard holding that post melts. ⚠️ **Trap:** the single-hot-row counter.
600	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** one logical counter can't absorb tens of thousands of writes/s.
601	
602	#### Good: Sharded counters
603	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad concentrated all increments on one row; Good spreads them across many sub-counters.
604	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Split the count into, say, 100 buckets; each like bumps a random bucket; the displayed total is the sum."
605	**Approach:** keep `post_id:shard_k` counters; a like increments a random shard; reads sum the shards. Increment load divides by the shard count.
606	**⚠️ What breaks:** reads must sum N shards each time (more read cost), and the total is eventually consistent across shards — fine for display, but you must serve the user's OWN like state separately.
607	
608	```mermaid
609	flowchart TD
610	    Like[Like event]
611	    Like -->|"1 incr random shard"| C1[(counter shard k)]
612	    Read[Display read] -->|"2 sum shards"| Agg[Aggregator]
613	    Agg -->|"3 read all shards"| C1
614	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
615	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
616	    class Like,Read,Agg svc
617	    class C1 db
618	```
619	
620	#### Great: Sharded counters + write-through cache + per-user idempotent set
621	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good still summed shards on every read and didn't make likes idempotent; Great caches the aggregate and dedups per user.
622	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Keep the buckets, but also cache the rolled-up total so reads are instant, and store each user's like as a set entry so liking twice counts once and you always see your own heart."
623	**Approach:** (1) likes go to an in-memory sharded counter (Redis `INCRBY` across shards), periodically flushed to durable storage. (2) A **write-through cache** holds the aggregate total, refreshed on a cadence — reads are O(1). (3) The user's like is stored as a member of a per-(user) or per-(post) set, making the like **idempotent** *(liking twice is the same as once)* and giving **read-your-own-writes** for the heart icon.
624	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** one hot row at ~30K likes/s = guaranteed lock death. Across 100 in-memory shards that's ~300/s each — trivial. The displayed total is approximate within one flush window; nobody can tell 1,200,300 from 1,200,000.
625	
626	**✅ Failure matrix**
627	| Scenario | What happens |
628	|---|---|
629	| Two likes from same user | Set dedups → counts once (idempotent) |
630	| User likes then reads | Reads own set → heart filled (read-your-own-writes) |
631	| Counter shard lost before flush | Lose a few un-flushed increments → count slightly low; reconciled from the like set |
632	| Viral spike | In-memory shards absorb; durable store updated in batches |
633	| Cache stale | Total off by < one flush window — acceptable for display |
634	
635	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A viral post would hammer a single counter row at tens of thousands of writes a second, so I never use one row. I shard the counter into ~100 in-memory buckets, each like bumps a random bucket, and the displayed total is the cached sum — approximate within a flush window, which is fine; nobody distinguishes 1.2 million from 1,200,300. Separately, I store each user's like as a set member, which makes liking idempotent and guarantees you always see your own heart filled even though the global count is eventual."
636	> 🧠 **If they ask "what if counts must be exact?":** "Then I'd flush more aggressively and reconcile from the durable like set, accepting higher write cost — but for a social feed, approximate display counts are the right trade."
637	
638	### How do we serve petabytes of media cheaply (upload pipeline + CDN)?
639	
640	#### Good: CDN in front of object storage
641	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Put copies of images at edge servers near users so most requests never reach our origin."
642	**Approach:** the **CDN** *(a network of edge caches near users that serves bytes locally)* fronts the object store; feed responses contain CDN URLs. ~95% of reads served from edge.
643	**⚠️ What breaks:** if we serve full-resolution originals, egress is enormous; and a cache miss on a popular new post can stampede the origin bucket.
644	
645	```mermaid
646	flowchart TD
647	    Client[Client app]
648	    Client -->|"1 GET image url"| CDN[CDN edge]
649	    CDN -.->|"2 miss to origin"| Blob[(Object store)]
650	    Blob -->|"3 fill edge"| CDN
651	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
652	    classDef ext fill:#2e1a3c,stroke:#6B46C1,color:#e6edf3
653	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
654	    class Client client
655	    class CDN ext
656	    class Blob db
657	```
658	
659	#### Great: Right-sized renditions + adaptive bitrate + lifecycle tiering
660	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Good served big originals; Great serves the smallest rendition that fits the slot and tiers cold media cheaply.
661	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Always send the small version sized for the screen slot, stream video at a bitrate that matches the network, and move old photos to cheap cold storage."
662	**Approach:** (1) transcode each upload into renditions (thumbnail / feed / full); the feed serves the ~0.3 MB feed rendition, not the ~5 MB original. (2) Video uses **adaptive bitrate** *(the player picks a quality that fits the current bandwidth)*. (3) **Lifecycle tiering** moves rarely-viewed media to cold object tiers. (4) CDN origin-shield + cache-key on rendition so misses collapse.
663	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** egress ≈ 10B reads/day x 10 posts/page x **0.3 MB** ≈ **30 PB/day → ~900 PB/month → ~$18M/mo** at ~$0.02/GB. Serving 5 MB originals instead would multiply egress ~16x — the difference between $18M and a quarter-billion a month. So renditions ARE the cost strategy.
664	
665	**✅ Failure matrix**
666	| Scenario | What happens |
667	|---|---|
668	| Edge cache hit | Served locally, no origin/egress-from-origin cost |
669	| Edge miss, popular post | Origin-shield collapses misses to one origin fetch |
670	| Transcode not done yet | Feed shows placeholder/original until rendition ready |
671	| Cold media requested | Pulled from cold tier (slightly slower first byte) then cached |
672	| Region CDN outage | DNS fails over to another CDN POP/provider |
673	
674	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Media is served by a CDN in front of object storage, so about 95% of bytes come from an edge near the user. The cost lever is renditions: the feed serves a roughly 0.3-megabyte version, never the 5-megabyte original — that one choice is the difference between an 18-million-a-month egress bill and something 16 times larger. Video uses adaptive bitrate, and old photos tier down to cheap cold storage. To stop a popular new post from stampeding origin, I use origin-shield so misses collapse to a single origin fetch."
675	> 🧠 **If they ask "biggest cost and how to cut it?":** "CDN egress, around $18M/mo. The biggest cut is aggressive rendition sizing and modern codecs — fewer bytes per view directly scales the bill."
676

678	
679	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
680	
681	### 9A — Availability targets
682	
683	- **Read/feed path: 99.99% (four nines)** = under ~52 minutes of downtime per year total. Mechanisms: CDN edge serving most media even if origin hiccups; Redis feed cache with replicas; **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centers in one region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically)* for the feed and post services; stale-feed-on-error fallback.
684	- **Write/upload path: 99.9% (three nines)** = under ~8.7 hours/year. Uploads can retry; durable queues mean a transient outage delays but never loses a post.
685	- Human terms: a reader almost never sees a broken feed; a poster occasionally sees "retry in a moment."
686	
687	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
688	
689	| Component | What breaks | Graceful degradation — what USER SEES | Recovery |
690	|---|---|---|---|
691	| CDN edge | Media stalls | Slightly slower image loads; failover to another POP/provider | DNS reroute; origin-shield absorbs |
692	| Object store (origin) | New renditions unavailable | Feed text + cached images still load; some images placeholder | Multi-region bucket replication |
693	| Feed cache (Redis) | Mailboxes gone | Brief latency spike; feed rebuilt from edges + posts | Rebuild on miss; warm from replica |
694	| Post metadata DB | Can't create posts / pull celebs | Reads of cached feed still work; posting shows retry | Replica promote; multi-AZ failover |
695	| Fan-out service/queue | Feeds stop updating | Feed shows slightly older posts | Durable queue drains on recovery; no loss |
696	| Counter store | Counts freeze | Like counts stop ticking; likes still recorded in set | Replay set / reconcile counters |
697	| Ranking scorer | No ranking | Feed falls back to chronological | Restart; degrade gracefully |
698	
699	### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
700	
701	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we're OK losing if it crashes right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is durably replicated before we ack. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until the service works again after a crash.
702	
703	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
704	|---|---|---|---|
705	| Media blobs | ≈0 | minutes | Object store w/ cross-region replication + versioning |
706	| Post metadata | seconds | minutes | Synchronous WAL + replica promotion; multi-AZ |
707	| Follow edges / likes | seconds | minutes | Multi-replica wide-column with quorum writes |
708	| Feed cache | N/A (derived) | seconds | Rebuildable from source — no backup needed |
709	| Counters | minutes (approx) | minutes | Reconcile from durable like set |
710	
711	### 9D — Cost breakdown
712	
713	1. **CDN egress — ~$18M/mo (the #1 line):** every media byte served to a device is billed; at ~900 PB/month this dwarfs everything. Why expensive: it's the product — people consume media constantly.
714	2. **Media storage at rest — ~64 PB/yr growing:** durable object storage, biggest after egress; lifecycle-tier cold media to cut it.
715	3. **Compute for fan-out + transcode + feed assembly:** large fleets, but an order of magnitude under egress.
716	4. **The single biggest optimization:** right-sized renditions + modern codecs (AV1/HEVC, WebP/AVIF) — cutting average bytes-per-view ~30-50% takes millions off the monthly egress bill directly.
717	
718	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the CDN hiccups, you just see images load a little slower — failover handles it. If the post DB has trouble, you can still scroll your cached feed but posting tells you to retry. If Redis loses your mailbox, there's a brief pause while we rebuild it from the follow graph. And if the counter store freezes, your like still registers in your own set — you see your heart fill — the public number just stops ticking for a moment. The big cost reality is that serving the images, around 18 million a month in CDN egress, is bigger than every database combined, so codecs and rendition sizing are where the money is."
719

722	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
723	
724	**🔀 Decision: Hybrid push/pull feed vs pure fan-out-on-write**
725	1. **Chose & why:** hybrid — push normal users, pull celebrities, because pure push can't survive a 400M-write celebrity post.
726	2. **Gave up:** read-time merge complexity and slightly higher feed-assembly latency for celebrity-heavy feeds.
727	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Pre-stuff most people's mailboxes, but fetch the famous folks live — like a mostly-prepared meal where we add the fresh garnish at serving time."
728	4. **When this reverses:** if no account ever exceeds a few hundred thousand followers (e.g. a private enterprise social network), drop pull entirely and go pure push — simpler.
729	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I went hybrid because the celebrity breaks pure push; I push normal users and pull the famous ones at read time."
730	
731	**🔀 Decision: Eventually-consistent feed & counts vs strong consistency**
732	1. **Chose & why:** eventual — the freshness SLO is seconds-to-minutes, which lets writes be cheap and async.
733	2. **Gave up:** a brief window where a new post or like isn't visible everywhere.
734	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We trade 'instantly perfect everywhere' for 'fast and fresh within seconds,' which nobody notices in a feed."
735	4. **When this reverses:** if this were a money/inventory system, I'd need strong consistency — but it's a social feed.
736	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Feeds and counts are eventually consistent on purpose; that's what keeps the write side cheap, and seconds of staleness is invisible here."
737	
738	**🔀 Decision: Direct-to-blob upload + async pipeline vs synchronous upload**
739	1. **Chose & why:** direct pre-signed upload + 202 — keeps petabytes off our servers and returns instantly.
740	2. **Gave up:** the post is briefly in PROCESSING; we validate/scan after the fact, not before.
741	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "The phone drops the photo straight in the warehouse and we tidy it up afterward, instead of carrying every photo through the front desk."
742	4. **When this reverses:** if strict pre-publish moderation were mandatory, I'd gate publish on a synchronous scan.
743	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Bytes go straight to object storage via a signed URL and we process async — it's the only way to handle this byte volume."
744	
745	**🔀 Decision: Sharded approximate counters vs exact single-source count**
746	1. **Chose & why:** sharded approximate counters — a single row can't take tens of thousands of likes/s on a viral post.
747	2. **Gave up:** exactness of the displayed total (off by < one flush window).
748	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We count likes in many buckets and add them up — the total is 'about right,' which is all a heart-count needs to be."
749	4. **When this reverses:** if a count drove billing or a contest, I'd flush aggressively and reconcile from the durable like set for exactness.
750	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Like counts are sharded and approximate so a viral post can't hot-row a single counter; your own like state is exact via a set."
751	
752	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The three decisions I'd defend hardest: hybrid feed over pure push, because the celebrity is a write bomb; eventual consistency over strong, because a feed only needs seconds of freshness and that keeps writes cheap; and direct-to-blob async upload, because you can't carry petabytes through your app servers. The one I'm least dogmatic about is approximate counters — if counts ever had to be exact, I'd reconcile from the durable like set and pay more on writes."
753

756	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
757	
758	**Core algorithm / hardest part**
759	
760	**❓ "Walk me through how a single home-feed read is served end to end."**
761	The mechanism: the feed service reads the user's pre-pushed Redis mailbox (a sorted set of post ids), issues one small live query for the user's followed celebrities (served from a shared per-celebrity cache), merges and ranks the candidates, then hydrates media URLs (not bytes) and returns. Reads are sub-ms on the mailbox plus a tiny merge.
762	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We read your pre-built mailbox, add the famous people's latest posts live, sort, and send back image links."
763	💬 **One-liner:** "Mailbox plus a live celebrity pull, ranked and merged — that's the whole read."
764	
765	**❓ "Why fan-out-on-write at all instead of just computing the feed on read?"**
766	The mechanism: reads outnumber writes ~300:1, so doing the expensive assembly on the rare write side and leaving reads as a cache fetch minimizes total work at 350K reads/s.
767	🗣️ **In plain words:** "People scroll way more than they post, so we do the hard work when they post, not when they scroll."
768	💬 **One-liner:** "300 reads to one write — so I pay the cost on the write side."
769	
770	**❓ "How does ranking fit without slowing the feed?"**
771	The mechanism: ranking is a pluggable scorer applied to an already-small candidate set (mailbox + celeb pull), so it re-orders tens-to-hundreds of items, not millions; if it's slow or down, we fall back to chronological.
772	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We rank a short list we already gathered, and if the ranker stalls we just show newest-first."
773	💬 **One-liner:** "Rank the candidates, not the corpus — and degrade to chronological."
774	
775	**Failure scenarios**
776	
777	**❓ "What happens if Redis (the feed cache) goes down?"**
778	The mechanism: feeds are a derived cache, so on loss we rebuild from the edge store + recent posts; replicas absorb most failures, and reads degrade to more pull while warming.
779	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Your mailbox is rebuildable, so worst case the feed is briefly slower while we refill it."
780	💬 **One-liner:** "It's a cache, not truth — we just rebuild it."
781	
782	**❓ "What if the object store / CDN origin is unavailable?"**
783	The mechanism: the CDN keeps serving cached media from the edge; new renditions may be delayed, and feed text/metadata still loads; cross-region bucket replication restores origin.
784	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Most images are already cached near you, so they keep loading even if the warehouse is briefly unreachable."
785	💬 **One-liner:** "The edge cache carries us through an origin blip."
786	
787	**❓ "What if a fan-out worker crashes mid-job?"**
788	The mechanism: the fan-out queue is durable and consumers are idempotent (re-pushing the same post id into a sorted set is a no-op), so the job resumes with at-least-once delivery and no duplicates or loss.
789	🗣️ **In plain words:** "The work is queued and safe to retry, so a crash just delays delivery."
790	💬 **One-liner:** "Durable queue plus idempotent push — retry, never lose."
791	
792	**Scale / hot-spot**
793	
794	**❓ "A celebrity with 400M followers posts — what happens?"**
795	The mechanism: we do NOT fan out on write; the post is served via read-time pull from a shared per-celebrity cache (single-flight on misses, replicated across cache nodes), so the post DB sees a handful of queries instead of 400M writes.
796	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We don't copy it 400 million times — everyone reads one shared cached copy when they open the app."
797	💬 **One-liner:** "Pull, don't push — one cached copy serves 400 million."
798	
799	**❓ "Flash crowd: a post goes viral and everyone likes it at once."**
800	The mechanism: likes hit ~100 in-memory sharded counters (random shard per like), so ~30K likes/s becomes ~300/s per shard; the displayed total is the cached sum, approximate within a flush window.
801	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We split the like counter into a hundred buckets so no single counter gets crushed."
802	💬 **One-liner:** "Shard the counter — no single hot row."
803	
804	**Consistency / correctness**
805	
806	**❓ "Can the same post be created twice on a retry?"**
807	The mechanism: `POST /posts` carries an idempotency key; the server dedups on it within a TTL window, returning the original postId on a retry.
808	🗣️ **In plain words:** "A flaky network retry won't double-post — we recognize the same request."
809	💬 **One-liner:** "Idempotency key — retries return the same post."
810	
811	**❓ "Do I see my own post / like immediately even though the feed is eventual?"**
812	The mechanism: read-your-own-writes — the author's own post is written strongly to their author shard and shown on their profile immediately, and a user's like is read from their own set, so the heart fills instantly even while the global count and others' feeds catch up.
813	🗣️ **In plain words:** "You always see your own actions right away; it's other people's view that's eventual."
814	💬 **One-liner:** "Eventual for the world, instant for you."
815	
816	**❓ "Can likes be double-counted?"**
817	The mechanism: a like is a set membership on `(user, post)`, so liking twice is idempotent — the count reflects unique likers, reconciled from the durable set if a counter shard is lost.
818	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Liking twice counts once because we store who liked, not just a number."
819	💬 **One-liner:** "Likes are a set — idempotent by construction."
820	
821	**Security**
822	
823	**❓ "How do you stop someone from posting as another user or forging fields?"**
824	The mechanism: the author id, timestamp, and status are all server-set from the JWT and server clock — never trusted from the request body; mutating endpoints require a valid token and authorization checks.
825	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We take who you are from your signed login token, not from what the app claims."
826	💬 **One-liner:** "Identity from the JWT, never from the body."
827	
828	**❓ "How do you keep the pre-signed upload URL from being abused?"**
829	The mechanism: the URL is scoped to one object key, a content-type, a max byte size, and a short expiry; after upload we validate size/type and async-scan before the post goes READY.
830	🗣️ **In plain words:** "The upload link only works for one file, briefly, and we check it afterward."
831	💬 **One-liner:** "Scoped, expiring, single-object signed URLs."
832	
833	**Cost**
834	
835	**❓ "What's the most expensive part and how would you cut it?"**
836	The mechanism: CDN egress (~$18M/mo at ~900 PB/month) dominates; the cut is right-sized renditions plus modern codecs (AVIF/WebP, AV1/HEVC) reducing bytes-per-view ~30-50%, directly scaling the bill.
837	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Serving the images is the big bill; sending smaller, better-compressed images is the biggest saving."
838	💬 **One-liner:** "Egress is the bill — codecs and renditions are the lever."
839	
840	**❓ "Isn't storing every photo forever expensive?"**
841	The mechanism: lifecycle tiering moves rarely-viewed media to cold object tiers at a fraction of the cost; metadata stays hot, bytes go cold.
842	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Old photos move to cheaper cold shelves automatically."
843	💬 **One-liner:** "Tier cold media down — hot metadata, cold bytes."
844	
845	**Extensibility**
846	
847	**❓ "How would you add Stories (ephemeral 24h posts)?"**
848	The mechanism: same upload pipeline, but a separate TTL'd store/feed structure (Redis with 24h expiry) and a parallel fan-out; expiry is free via TTL, and they render in a separate tray, not the main feed.
849	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Same upload, but they auto-expire after a day and live in their own row."
850	💬 **One-liner:** "Reuse upload + fan-out, add a 24-hour TTL."
851	
852	**❓ "How would you add an Explore / discovery feed?"**
853	The mechanism: it's a different candidate source — popularity/embedding-based retrieval rather than the follow graph — feeding the same ranking + media-hydration layer, so the read path is reused with a new candidate generator.
854	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Same machinery, but the candidates come from 'what's popular for you,' not who you follow."
855	💬 **One-liner:** "Swap the candidate source, reuse the ranker and CDN."
856	
857	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "Instagram is a read-heavy, media-heavy social network where people post photos and short videos, follow others, and scroll a personalized home feed. The design splits into two halves. The read half serves media from a CDN in front of object storage — 95% from the edge with right-sized renditions to keep egress sane — and serves the feed from pre-built Redis mailboxes, merging in a live pull of the celebrities you follow, then ranking the short candidate list. The write/transaction half takes uploads directly to blob storage via pre-signed URLs, transcodes asynchronously, and fans posts out into follower mailboxes; the hardest problem in it is the celebrity, whose single post would be 400 million writes — solved by never pushing celebrities and instead pulling them at read time from a shared, single-flight cache. The two halves are joined by a durable fan-out queue that lets the cheap write side pre-compute everything the read side needs. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: reads outnumber writes ~300 to 1, so push all the expensive work onto the write side and keep reads a cache fetch."
